title: Centralized Audit Event Logging Policy Coverage

compliance.assurance_rationale: Auto-migrated from cat-22 markdown on Phase 1.3 run. Requires SME review to confirm clause accuracy, mode (satisfies vs detects-violation-of), and assurance level (contributing/partial/full).
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIST 800-53 AU-2 (Event logging) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.14.1: Centralized Audit Event Logging Policy Coverage.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.14.1' running on index=sec OR index=os — Windows Security, Linux auditd, cloud control planes (sourcetypes vary by TA), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

dataSources: `index=sec` OR `index=os` — Windows Security, Linux auditd, cloud control planes (sourcetypes vary by TA)
app: Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (Splunkbase 1621)
spl:
index IN (sec, os, cloud) earliest=-24h
| stats dc(host) as hosts, dc(sourcetype) as sourcetypes, count as events by index, sourcetype
| lookup nist_au2_event_coverage.csv sourcetype OUTPUT au2_event_family, required
| eval gap=if(required="true" AND events=0, "No_Data", "OK")
| sort index, sourcetype
description: Shows which systems and sourcetypes are forwarding security-relevant events so auditors can evidence that logging policy scope matches AU-2 event types.
value: Shows which systems and sourcetypes are forwarding security-relevant events so auditors can evidence that logging policy scope matches AU-2 event types.

visualization: Table (coverage by sourcetype), Single value (percent required sources reporting), Bar chart (event volume by family)

knownFalsePositives: Administrative tasks, scheduled jobs or platform updates can match this pattern — correlate with change management, maintenance windows and user role before raising severity.
grandmaExplanation: Shows which systems and log type are forwarding security-relevant events so auditors can evidence that logging policy scope matches AU-2 event types — so you can show exactly what is being watched and when something went wrong.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (Splunkbase 1621).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=sec` OR `index=os` — Windows Security, Linux auditd, cloud control planes (sourcetypes vary by TA).
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Maintain `nist_au2_event_coverage.csv` mapping sourcetypes to AU-2 event families; (2) schedule daily and alert on sourcetypes with `required=true` and zero volume; (3) onboard missing forwarders or enable missing channels; (4) attach evidence export for assessors.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index IN (sec, os, cloud) earliest=-24h
| stats dc(host) as hosts, dc(sourcetype) as sourcetypes, count as events by index, sourcetype
| lookup nist_au2_event_coverage.csv sourcetype OUTPUT au2_event_family, required
| eval gap=if(required="true" AND events=0, "No_Data", "OK")
| sort index, sourcetype
```

Understanding this SPL

**Centralized Audit Event Logging Policy Coverage (AU-2)** — Shows which systems and sourcetypes are forwarding security-relevant events so auditors can evidence that logging policy scope matches AU-2 event types.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=sec` OR `index=os` — Windows Security, Linux auditd, cloud control planes (sourcetypes vary by TA). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (Splunkbase 1621). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by index, sourcetype** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
• Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
• `eval` defines or adjusts **gap** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Optional CIM / accelerated variant (same use case, normalized fields via Common Information Model):

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication by Authentication.action, Authentication.user, Authentication.src | sort - count
```

Understanding this CIM / accelerated SPL

**Centralized Audit Event Logging Policy Coverage (AU-2)** — Shows which systems and sourcetypes are forwarding security-relevant events so auditors can evidence that logging policy scope matches AU-2 event types.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=sec` OR `index=os` — Windows Security, Linux auditd, cloud control planes (sourcetypes vary by TA). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (Splunkbase 1621). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

This **CIM or accelerated** block uses normalized field names and/or `tstats` over data models. Enable **acceleration** on the referenced models (and correct CIM knowledge objects) or the search may return nothing.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Authentication.Authentication` — enable acceleration for that model.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Enable Data Model Acceleration (and metric indexes for `mstats`) for the models or datasets referenced above; otherwise `tstats`/`mstats` may return no results from summaries.


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (coverage by sourcetype), Single value (percent required sources reporting), Bar chart (event volume by family)
